channel.title: Secondary Meaning
channel.description: Understanding the laws that protect brands.

item.title: 360 Deals: Considerations For An Artist's Long-Term Income
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
The way artists make money in today’s music industry has changed drastically. Album sales no longer dominate revenue streams. Instead, different buckets like touring, merchandise, streaming, endorsements, and licensing often represent a larger share of an artist’s income. In response, many record labels offer 360 deals: contracts that give the label a percentage of nearly every revenue stream an artist generates. This article analyzes the effects of 360 deals on an artist’s long-term earnings. 

What Is a 360 Deal?

A 360 deal is a type of record contract where the label receives a portion of the artist's income in multiple areas beyond just music sales. This may include touring, merchandise, publishing, sponsorships, and even digital revenue. Traditionally, labels earned primarily from album sales. However, the decline in physical sales and the rise of streaming prompted the adoption of these comprehensive “360” agreements. Although percentages vary by artist, labels may take anywhere from 10% to 30% of non-recording income streams.

Benefits of 360 Deals

For emerging artists, 360 deals can provide significant advantages. First, they can give artists access to additional resources, as labels often provide marketing, tour support, and brand development that might otherwise be out of reach. Likewise, labels may offer increased financial support, such as higher upfront advances to cover recording costs, tours, and promotional campaigns. Greater investment in the artist and leveraging the label’s expertise and connections can result in more exposure and faster career growth.

Risks and Drawbacks

Despite the benefits, 360 deals are not without trade-offs. Giving a cut of every income stream can significantly reduce long-term earnings, particularly from tours and merchandise, which are often highly profitable. Additionally, an artist often forfeits autonomy in exchange for a 360 deal, as the label exercises more influence on decisions about touring, partnerships, or merchandising. And for mid-tier or established artists, the label’s percentage may outweigh the benefits, especially if the artist’s brand grows stronger independently.

Negotiating a 360 Deal

For artists considering a 360 deal, legal guidance becomes even more important. Artists should pay attention to the scope of revenue streams the label wants to participate in, since not all streams need to be included. For example, many artists successfully negotiate to exclude merchandise, digital revenue, or other high-value areas. Recoupment clauses also deserve careful scrutiny, as they determine how advances are recovered across multiple income sources and can significantly affect long-term earnings. Additionally, counsel should evaluate the duration and territory of the agreement to ensure the deal doesn’t bind the artist to restrictive terms for an unreasonable period. For some artists, a partial 360 deal, where the label participates in only select revenue streams, can strike a better balance by limiting the label’s percentage to certain areas, allowing the artist to maintain full control over their most important revenue sources while still benefiting from the label’s support.

Long-Term Financial Implications

The upshot is that 360 deals can be a double-edged sword. While they offer resources and financial backing that can launch an artist’s career, they also involve sharing a significant portion of revenue. Artists must weigh the value of immediate support against the potential cost of long-term earnings. Artists with enough leverage may be able to mitigate some of the downside by retaining control over high-value streams, such as merchandise or publishing.

Conclusion

A 360 deal is not inherently bad, but it is not automatically good either. Artists should carefully evaluate their current career stage, projected growth, and the label’s track record before deciding whether a 360 deal makes sense for the artist’s short-term needs and long-term income potential.

item.title: Advances Explained: Why “Free Money” Isn’t Free
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
Signing a record deal is an exciting milestone for most artists. One of the most appealing aspects is the advance: an upfront payment from the label intended to cover recording costs, marketing, and other expenses. But what seems like free money can quickly become a financial trap if artists don’t fully understand how advances are recovered.

What Is an Advance?

An advance is an upfront payment to an artist meant to fund the costs of creating and promoting music before the project generates any revenue. Usually paid by a record label, distributor, or streaming service, an advance may cover recording sessions, producers, mixing and mastering, videos, tour support, and sometimes even the artist’s personal living expenses while working on the project.

Advances are recoupable, meaning the artist is expected to pay back the money from future earnings from record sales, streaming royalties, or other revenue streams covered in the contract. That means the artist generally won’t receive additional royalty payments until the label has fully recouped the amount of the advance. But unlike a traditional loan, the artist generally does not have to pay back an advance back out of pocket if their earnings don’t cover the advance. The payor of the advance recovers it (if at all) only from the artist’s share of revenue streams outlined in the contract.

Understanding Recoupment

When an artist receives an advance, the payor of that advance, usually a label, expects to recover that money from the artist’s future income. As the artist earns revenue from streaming, sales, or other sources, the artist’s share of those royalties is applied toward paying back the advance. The contract will spell out which earnings count toward recoupment, how they’re calculated, and when the artist is considered fully recouped, but only after the advance has been paid back does the artist start receiving royalty checks.

For example, if an artist receives a $50,000 advance and earns $1 per album sold, the first $50,000 of album sales (or equivalent revenue from other covered streams) goes entirely toward paying back the label. Once the label fully recovers the $50,000, the artist is officially “recouped,” at which point the artist keeps royalties generated from album sales. 

Cross-Collaterization

Cross-collateralization is a standard but often misunderstood feature of advances, and it can dramatically affect how and when an artist becomes recouped. When an advance is “cross-collaterized,” the label can use earnings from one project or revenue stream to cover the unrecouped balance of another. In other words, the artist’s various projects under the same deal are financially linked, such that success in one area can be used to offset losses in another.

For example, if an artist receives separate advances for two albums and only one album performs well, the label can apply the royalties from the successful album toward recouping the advances for both releases. The artist doesn’t begin receiving royalties from the profitable project until the combined advances have been fully repaid.

This arrangement can delay or reduce an artist’s earnings, especially when one project underperforms. To avoid that, artists and their legal advisors must understand how cross-collateralization works and consider negotiating limitations or exceptions when possible so that one weak project doesn’t undermine the financial return of a successful one. That said, artists without sufficient bargaining power are unlikely to successfully limit cross-collateralization. 

Avoiding Common Pitfalls With Advances

Artists can find themselves in financial trouble by misunderstanding the economics of their deals. This might occur when an artist takes a large advance without any realistic sense of whether their music can generate enough revenue to pay it back. The upfront money is appealing, but if sales or streams don’t hit the targets the label is expecting, the artist could spend years trying to recoup. The problem is exacerbated when artists don’t fully grasp which expenses are recoupable. Many contracts permit recoupment of things like music videos, tour support, and broad marketing costs, which can balloon the amount the artist owes. And if the artist has entered into a 360 deal, a label may be able to pull from even more income streams to repay themselves before the artist sees any real money.

Artists can avoid this pitfall by approaching the deal realistically and negotiating terms that protect their future earnings. For example, a smaller advance with more limitations on what the label can recoup may be safer than a larger number with hidden strings. Knowing exactly which expenses will be charged back, and pushing to exclude high-cost items, can sharply reduce long-term risk. Retaining control over profitable revenue streams, such as merchandise, also helps keep money out of the recoupment pool. Finally, artists should create a clear financial plan to manage expenses while the advance is being repaid. This could mean setting aside a portion of the advance for essential expenses, cutting back on nonessential spending, or finding additional ways to earn income until they are fully recouped and royalties start flowing.

Conclusion

Advances can provide important funding for an artist’s creative projects, but without careful planning, it may take years before royalties start flowing. Understanding the mechanics of recoupment, negotiating favorable terms, and retaining control over key revenue streams are essential steps to ensure that an artist benefits financially from their work, rather than having their earnings tied up for an extended period by the recoupment process.

item.title: What Is a Trademark?
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
Understanding Trademarks and How They Differ from Copyrights and Patents

Intellectual property law protects different kinds of creations in different ways. A common source of confusion is the distinction between trademarks, copyrights, and patents. This article explains, at a basic level, what a trademark is and why it’s fundamentally different from copyrights and patents.

Trademarks

A trademark is any word, name, symbol, sound, color, or smell that identifies the source of goods or services and distinguishes them from others. The legal definition appears in Section 45 of the Lanham Act (15 U.S.C. § 1127), but the general idea is much simpler: a trademark tells the consumer, “This came from me.”

Examples of trademarks include the word STARBUCKS on a cup of coffee, the Nike swoosh on a sneaker, or the distinctive blue box from Tiffany & Co. Consumers instantly connect each of these to a single source, and trademark law exists to protect that connection.

Contrasting Trademarks With Copyrights and Patents

Copyright and patent law, on the other hand, serve entirely different purposes. Copyright protects original works of authorship that are fixed in a tangible medium: novels, songs, photographs, software code, architectural drawings, and the like. The moment an author finishes writing a story or a programmer saves a new piece of code, copyright protection automatically attaches to that work. The primary goal of copyright protection is to prevent unauthorized copying and to give creators control over reproduction, distribution, public performance, and derivative works.

Patent law protects inventions: new and useful processes, machines, manufactures, or compositions of matter (as well as new ornamental designs). A patent grants its owner the right to exclude others from making, using, or selling the invention for a limited time. Unlike copyright, patent protection is never automatic; it requires a rigorous examination by the United States Patent and Trademark Office.

The differences in duration between trademark protection, on the one hand, and copyrights and patents, on the other, are key. Copyright protection lasts for the life of the author plus seventy years (or ninety-five to one hundred twenty years for works made for hire). Patents expire after twenty years from the filing date.

Trademarks, however, can last indefinitely as long as the owner continues to use the mark, renews its registration (if registered), and defends it against infringement or dilution. Trademark protection can last forever because its sole purpose is to prevent ongoing consumer confusion in the marketplace. As long as the mark is actively used and continues to identify a single source, society still benefits from that protection. Copyright and patent rights, however, are temporary monopolies granted for a limited time to incentivize creation and disclosure of new works and inventions, after which they must enter the public domain so everyone can freely build upon them.

How Trademark Rights Arise

Trademark rights can arise in two ways. First, simply by using a mark in commerce, a business can acquire common-law rights in the geographic area(s) where the business actually uses the mark. No filing is required, and the rights continue as long as the business keeps selling under that mark and the mark does not become generic.

Second, a business can apply for federal registration with the United States Patent and Trademark Office. Federal registration requires continued use as well. Once the registration issues, the owner enjoys nationwide priority, a legal presumption of validity after five years of continuous registration, and powerful additional remedies in court. The owner must renew the registration every ten years thereafter, but as long as the owner continues to use the mark, renews its registration, and defends it against infringement and genericide, the owner can maintain the registration indefinitely. 

Conclusion

A trademark, at its essence, is about source identification and consumer protection. It is a legal mechanism that gives consumers confidence that the product or service they are buying comes from the source they expect. Moreover, it gives businesses the exclusive right to that trust.

Unlike copyrights and patents, which expire so that creative works and inventions can eventually belong to everyone, trademarks can last forever. As long as the mark continues to function as a reliable indicator of source and the owner actively uses and defends it, the law recognizes that consumer recognition and the goodwill tied to a brand have no natural expiration.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: The Cost of Ignoring Music Licenses: Behr's Case
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
In November 2025, ABKCO Music & Records, Inc. (owner of the Rolling Stones’ pre-1971 catalog) filed a federal copyright infringement lawsuit against Behr Paint in the Central District of California. The claim stems from a 15-second Instagram Reel posted by Behr that used the original 1966 Rolling Stones recording of “Paint It, Black” to promote a new line of black paint. No synchronization license and no master-use license were obtained.

The Lawsuit

In late October 2025, Behr posted an Instagram Reel promoting their new shades of black paint. To match the edgy vibe, Behr played the full original 1966 recording of “Paint It, Black,” while showing paint slathered on walls in dramatic black. According to the complaint, Behr never requested or obtained a synchronization license for the underlying musical composition or a master-use license for the sound recording itself. The Reel garnered thousands of views before Behr took it down, just hours after receiving a cease-and-desist letter from ABKCO.

ABKCO alleges direct copyright infringement and characterizes the violation as willful, pointing out that Behr is a multi-billion-dollar company (owned by Masco and closely tied to Home Depot) that routinely licenses well-known music for its advertising campaigns and therefore cannot credibly claim ignorance of the licensing process. In the alternative, the complaint asserts vicarious and contributory infringement against Behr and unnamed Doe defendants (likely the agency or production partners).

Although Behr removed the Reel within hours of receiving ABKCO’s cease-and-desist letter, ABKCO is pressing forward with the case, arguing that the unauthorized commercial use created an unwanted brand association and deprived the rights holders of licensing revenue they otherwise would have earned. ABKCO is seeking the full range of remedies available under the Copyright Act: actual damages and disgorgement of Behr’s profits attributable to the infringement (17 U.S.C. § 504(b)), or alternatively the maximum statutory damages for willful infringement (up to $150,000 per work under § 504(c)), plus attorneys’ fees, costs, and prejudgment interest.

Takeaways for Brand and Marketing Teams

  1. Two separate licenses are always required to use a famous original recording: Music copyright is divided into two distinct rights: the musical composition (lyrics + melody) and the sound recording (the specific studio master). Using the actual Rolling Stones recording of “Paint It, Black” legally requires (a) a synchronization (“sync”) license from the music publisher and (b) a master-use license from the owner of the recording (ABKCO in this case). Securing only one of these licenses (or none at all) is still infringement.
  1. Stock-music and subscription libraries do not include major-label masters: Platforms such as Epidemic Sound, Artlist, AudioJungle, MusicBed, APM, Killer Tracks, and similar services are excellent for royalty-free or pre-cleared production music, but they never contain the original studio recordings of superstar artists released by Universal, Sony, Warner, or legacy catalogs like ABKCO. You will find only newly created tracks or cover versions in those libraries. There is no legal mechanism for those services to offer the real 1966 Rolling Stones master (or virtually any classic-rock radio staple) at scale. Assuming otherwise is a common and expensive mistake.
  1. “We took it down right away” is not a defense: Duration and speed of removal can sometimes reduce damages and demonstrate good faith, but they do not eliminate liability. Federal courts regularly award significant statutory damages for brief, commercial uses of even a few seconds of a famous recording when the infringement is deemed willful.
  1. Always get written confirmation of exactly which rights were cleared: The single most common cause of an infringement lawsuit like this one is a breakdown in the chain of responsibility: the agency clears the composition but assumes the brand will handle the master, the social team uses the original Spotify rip because “it sounded better,” or a freelancer says “the music is licensed” without specifying which rights. Companies can eliminate the ambiguity by requiring every vendor, agency, or contractor to confirm in writing (before the content is published) that both the sync license and the master-use license have been obtained for the exact recording used, and to attach copies of both approvals. If they cannot provide both documents, do not post the work.

Clearing music properly at the outset almost always costs a fraction of what a federal copyright lawsuit will cost to litigate or settle. The Behr case is a reminder that even large, sophisticated companies can make costly mistakes when they skip the music-licensing step.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Practical Considerations Before Suing for Trademark Infringement
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
Filing a trademark infringement lawsuit can be a powerful way to protect a brand, but it shouldn’t be undertaken without carefully weighing the strategic and practical factors that determine whether litigation is worthwhile. This article outlines key considerations to review before taking action.

1. Ensure Priority (First to Use)

In the U.S., trademark rights generally go to the party who first uses a mark in commerce. Establishing priority often requires clear documentation of when and how the mark was first used, along with evidence of continuous use. If another party claims concurrent use, the issue may become more complex, particularly in overlapping geographic or product markets. Before filing suit, it’s essential to assess whether you have a strong claim as the first user of the mark. Ensuring priority is critical, because failing to establish it could expose you to a counterclaim for infringement, effectively acknowledging the similarity of the marks.

2. Strength of the Mark

The strength of a mark plays a major role in any infringement analysis. Unsurprisingly, strong marks are easier to enforce and more likely to result in favorable outcomes. Prospective plaintiffs should consider the following factors when evaluating the strength of a mark:

  • Registration: Federal registration provides presumptive nationwide rights and constructive notice to others. Unregistered marks rely on common law rights, which are geographically limited.
  • Incontestability: Marks that have achieved incontestable status enjoy protection against certain challenges, such as descriptiveness claims.
  • Distinctiveness: Arbitrary and fanciful marks are strongest. Suggestive marks are moderately strong. Descriptive marks must have acquired secondary meaning to qualify for protection.
  • Fame and Consumer Recognition: Well-known or famous marks may receive broader protection, including against dilution or similar uses even in unrelated markets.

A weak or descriptive mark may be enforceable, but litigation is riskier and often more expensive relative to likely remedies.

3. Gaps in Scope of Protection

Even strong marks have limits. Before suing, consider whether there are gaps in protection that could affect your case. These gaps might include:

  • Geographic Scope: For unregistered marks, rights may be limited to areas where the mark is actively used.
  • Continuity of Use: Inconsistent or abandoned use can undermine a claim.
  • Industry or Product-Specific Limitations: Rights typically extend only to the goods or services for which the mark is used.

Understanding these limitations helps set realistic expectations for what a lawsuit can achieve.

4. Good Faith vs. Bad Faith Intent

The alleged infringer’s intent can influence remedies and damages, not to mention the public’s perception of the dispute. Courts may consider whether the infringer knowingly copied your mark or acted in bad faith. Although unintentional use does not necessarily defeat an infringement claim, clear evidence of copying or deliberate confusion can strengthen your position, both in court and in the court of public opinion.

5. Potential Defenses

Even if your mark is strong and priority is clear, defendants may raise defenses that could limit or defeat your claim. Assessing these in advance helps you determine the strength of your case and prepare evidence accordingly:

  • Fair Use: Descriptive fair use occurs when the alleged infringer uses the mark in good faith to describe their own goods or services rather than to indicate source. For example, using the word “apple” to describe the fruit rather than the tech company. Nominative fair use allows limited use of a mark to refer to the mark owner’s goods or services, often for comparative advertising or commentary. Both forms can be valid defenses if the use is reasonable and not misleading.
  • Abandonment: As mentioned above, a mark that has not been used consistently or has been deliberately abandoned may no longer be enforceable. Evidence of discontinuous use or intentional non-use can be a strong defense.
  • Laches or Acquiescence: Delay can hurt your case. If you knew about the use and waited too long to enforce your rights, the defendant can argue that the delay caused prejudice. Although laches doesn’t always bar injunctive relief, it can limit damages. Relatedly, acquiescence applies when the owner’s conduct led the defendant to reasonably believe the owner consented to the use. Suggesting collaboration, failing to object during negotiations, or giving mixed signals can fuel this defense.
  • Estoppel: If your statements or conduct misled the defendant into believing they could use the mark, you may be estopped from claiming infringement. This can occur through silence in situations where you had a clear duty to speak, or through prior dealings.

6. Other Practical Considerations

Litigation is costly and time-consuming. Before committing to a lawsuit, it’s worth taking a hard look at whether the juice is worth the squeeze. In many cases, the litigation costs can exceed the likely recovery, especially if damages are uncertain or the infringing use is already tapering off.

Potential plaintiffs should also consider the likelihood of obtaining injunctive relief. Courts are far more willing to issue injunctions when the infringement is clear, ongoing, and likely to mislead consumers. But the expected value of litigation may drop sharply if the case is borderline or the alleged infringer has already backed down.

Finally, one must consider the potential impact on your brand and public reputation. Litigation can sometimes create a Barbra Streisand effect, causing more harm and attention than the underlying infringement ever did. Potential plaintiffs must think about whether a public fight aligns with broader business goals and how it might affect customer relationships, industry partners, or media perception.

In short, not every dispute needs to be litigated. Demand letters, structured negotiations, or mediation often resolve conflicts at a fraction of the cost. Exploring these options early can preserve resources and avoid the unpredictability of the courtroom.

Conclusion

Trademark infringement lawsuits can be powerful tools, but success depends on more than proving infringement. Assessing priority, mark strength, scope of protection, intent, potential defenses, and practical implications will help you decide whether litigation is the right strategy. Thoughtful preparation not only increases your chances of a favorable outcome but can also save time, money, and reputational risk.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Why “Softsoap” Almost Never Became a Registered Trademark: Understanding Descriptive Marks and the Path to Protection
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
When creating brand names, many entrepreneurs wish to use words that describe what the product does, such as “Pure Water,” or “Soft Soap.” Although this impulse is understandable, it can cause issues when applying for federal trademark registration.

The Five Categories of Marks

The United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) and federal courts evaluate trademarks on a spectrum of distinctiveness. See, e.g., Abercrombie & Fitch Co. v. Hunting World, Inc., 537 F.2d 4, 9 (2d Cir. 1976).  Marks are placed into one of the following categories:

  1. Generic: the common name of the product itself (e.g., “Water” for bottled water). Generic marks are never protectable.
  2. Descriptive: immediately tells the consumer what the product is or does, without requiring any imagination, by conveying an ingredient, quality, characteristic, feature, function, or purpose of the goods (e.g., “Softsoap,” “Crunchy Cereal”). Descriptive marks are eligible for registration only after acquiring “secondary meaning,” as discussed below.
  3. Suggestive: requires a step of imagination to connect the mark to the goods (e.g., “Coppertone” for sunscreen). Suggestive marks are considered inherently distinctive and protectable.
  4. Arbitrary: an existing word applied in an unrelated context (e.g., “Apple” for computers). Arbitrary marks are also inherently distinctive and receive strong protection.
  5. Fanciful: invented words (e.g., “Kodak,” “Xerox”). Fanciful marks, which are also inherently distinctive, are the strongest marks.

Importantly, descriptive marks are a border case between unprotectable generic terms and inherently distinctive marks. From the USPTO’s perspective, no single seller should be allowed to monopolize ordinary language that competitors need to simply describe their own identical or similar products. Allowing one company to immediately own “Soft Soap” would make it nearly impossible for competitors to tell consumers their soap is also soft. Thus, descriptive marks are initially refused registration unless and until they acquire secondary meaning—i.e., consumers have come to recognize the term as identifying a single brand rather than just describing the product.

This principle explains Soft Soap’s seven-year fight to secure federal registration, discussed below.

The Softsoap Saga

In 1980, Minnetonka, Inc. launched SOFTSOAP, the first mass-market liquid hand soap in a pump dispenser. The product quickly achieved massive sales through heavy advertising and exclusive pump supply control. 

Prior to the product’s launch, the USPTO refused Minnetonka’s trademark application on the ground that “Softsoap” was merely descriptive (or highly descriptive bordering on generic) of liquid soap. See In re Minnetonka, Inc., 212 USPQ 772, 782 (TTAB 1981). After filing a civil action pursuant to Section 21(b), Minnetonka refiled its application in December 1983 with extensive proof of secondary meaning, including affidavits, consumer surveys, advertising materials and evidence of exclusive use. On April 9, 1987, the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board (TTAB) reversed the refusal, concluding that while highly descriptive, “SOFTSOAP” had come to identify Minnetonka as the single source in consumers’ minds. The mark registered on the Principal Register on September 22, 1987 (U.S. Reg. No. 1,457,886). Thus, this case is a classic example of how descriptive terms can achieve full protection through strong evidence of acquired distinctiveness.

Many now-familiar marks followed a similar path. For instance, American Airlines began as geographically descriptive, simply indicating an airline operating in or from the United States. But through decades of exclusive use and nationwide advertising since the 1930s, consumers eventually came to associate the mark solely with one carrier, allowing it to acquire secondary meaning and secure strong federal protection. Sharp, the television brand, was originally descriptive of picture quality: a “sharp” image. But over years of consistent branding and market presence, the term shifted in consumers’ minds to identify products from Sharp Corporation specifically, transforming it into a protectable source indicator. Finally, Holiday Inn, the hotel chain, started as a purely descriptive phrase suggesting lodgings suitable for holidays or vacations. Persistent nationwide use, iconic signage, and heavy promotion starting in the 1950s built exclusive recognition, enabling the mark to acquire secondary meaning and achieve registration on the Principal Register.

These examples illustrate that descriptive marks, though initially weak and often refused registration, can acquire secondary meaning through extensive use, advertising, and proven consumer recognition. This process ultimately grants them the same strong protection enjoyed by inherently distinctive marks.

Practical Advice for Brands

  1. Prioritize suggestive, arbitrary, or fanciful marks from the start. Names like “Dove” (suggestive of gentleness and purity for soap) or “Apple” (arbitrary for computers) are inherently distinctive and typically register quickly without extra evidence or cost. In contrast, descriptive terms like “Softsoap” face initial refusals and can require years of use, substantial proof of secondary meaning, additional filings, and higher legal expenses.
  1. If a descriptive name is unavoidable, plan for the long road to full protection. Budget time and resources for extensive marketing, consistent and substantially exclusive use, and gathering robust evidence of acquired distinctiveness (e.g., sales data, advertising expenditures, consumer surveys, and declarations). This evidence is often essential to overcome a Section 2(f) refusal and move the mark to the Principal Register.
  1. Use dual or composite branding to maximize immediate protection. Pair a descriptive term with a distinctive house mark, logo, or stylized element (e.g., OWALA FreeSip, STANLEY Quencher, or DOVE with its distinctive swan logo). The distinctive component can often register right away, providing enforceable rights while the descriptive portion builds secondary meaning over time.

Key Takeaway

Descriptive marks are not barred from protection forever, but they begin on the weakest footing and face significant hurdles to registration on the Principal Register. The USPTO requires substantial evidence that the term has acquired secondary meaning and now functions as a true source identifier in consumers’ minds. Proving this often takes years of exclusive use, heavy advertising, and market dominance, all before the mark can be registered.

The Softsoap saga, along with familiar brands like American Airlines, Sharp, and Holiday Inn, demonstrate that this transformation is possible with persistence and marketplace success. However, these stories also serve as a powerful reminder that the safest, fastest, and most cost-effective route to immediate trademark protection almost always lies in choosing a suggestive, arbitrary, or fanciful name that sparks a bit of imagination from the very start.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Navigating the 2025 USPTO Trademark Fee Changes
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
The United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) introduced major adjustments to trademark fees on January 18, 2025, with some Madrid Protocol-related changes effective February 18, 2025. These updates mark the first significant revision in several years and reflect the agency’s efforts to recover rising operational costs, incentivize efficient filing practices, and fund initiatives under its 2022–2026 Strategic Plan. 

Key Fee Changes

The changes eliminate the previous two-tiered filing system—TEAS Plus at $250 per class and TEAS Standard at $350 per class. Replacing it is a unified base application fee of $350 per class for applications under Sections 1 (use-based or intent-to-use) and 44 (foreign-based) of the Trademark Act. Although this simplifies the process by removing strict TEAS Plus requirements, it raises the entry cost for many applicants who previously qualified for the lower tier.

To encourage completeness and standardization, the USPTO introduced new surcharges. A $100 per-class fee now applies if applications lack essential information, such as the applicant’s name, domicile address, entity type, translations for non-English terms, or detailed descriptions for design marks. More significantly, applicants must use pre-approved identifications of goods and services from the USPTO’s Trademark ID Manual to avoid extra costs. Opting for custom free-form text incurs a $200 per-class surcharge for the first 1,000 characters, with an additional $200 for each subsequent 1,000 characters. These length-based fees are intended to discourage overly verbose descriptions that extend examination time.

Post-registration fees also rose to offset increased processing demands, particularly for reviewing specimens of use. Declarations under Section 8 (affirming continued use, required between years 5–6 and with renewals) jumped from $225 to $325 per class. Section 9 renewal applications (required every 10 years) increased from $300 to $325 per class, while Section 15 declarations of incontestability rose from $200 to $250 per class. Fees for amendments to allege use or statements of use now cost $150 per class, up from $100, reflecting higher scrutiny of “questionable” specimens.

Other notable hikes include petitions to the Director (from $250 to $400), petitions to revive abandoned applications (from $150 to $250), and letters of protest by third parties (from $50 to $150).

Impacts on Trademark Filings 

These adjustments carry broad implications for trademark strategy. The USPTO anticipates that most filers will pay only the $350 base fee by submitting complete applications with ID Manual descriptions, potentially speeding up examinations and benefiting the system overall. However, applicants with complex or unique goods/services, which are common in tech, fashion, or software sectors, may see costs soar, as surcharges can add hundreds or thousands per application in multi-class cases.

Small businesses, startups, and individual filers could feel disproportionate impacts, as the loss of the $250 option and new penalties for customization raise barriers to entry. In addition, large portfolio owners will also be affected due to maintenance costs that are likely to increase. As a result, they will more carefully review unused registrations to avoid unnecessary renewals.

On the upside, the new structure incentivizes best practices. Thorough preparation should reduce office actions, and standardized filings could shorten waiting times. The changes also aim to deter low-quality or fraudulent applications that clog the register.

Strategies to Navigate Fee Changes

Penalties can often be avoided by relying heavily on the Trademark ID Manual for goods and services descriptions and limiting free-form language to situations where it is genuinely necessary. Applicants should also edit aggressively to remain within the 1,000-character limit per class and ensure all required applicant information is collected upfront to avoid surcharges. For intent-to-use applications, confirming a realistic path to commercialization before filing is now increasingly important. Regular portfolio audits can further reduce costs by identifying registrations that no longer justify ongoing maintenance.

Consulting experienced trademark counsel remains advisable, especially for complex identifications or international filings, as professional guidance can prevent costly surcharges and office actions.

Conclusion

The 2025 fee revisions balance fiscal sustainability with incentives for quality, aiming for a more reliable and efficient U.S. trademark ecosystem. While costs rise in targeted areas, proactive compliance can keep expenses manageable and yield faster, stronger protections. Always check the official USPTO fee schedule for the latest details.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Likelihood of Confusion Isn’t One Test: Why Circuit Differences in Trademark Law Actually Matter
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
A key element of any trademark infringement claim is the likelihood of confusion. A plaintiff must show that the defendant’s use of a mark is likely to confuse ordinary consumers as to the source, sponsorship, affiliation, or approval of the goods or services. Although federal courts generally agree on this ultimate inquiry, they apply different multi-factor tests depending on the circuit. These differences are often described as minor or semantic, but in practice they can meaningfully affect outcomes, litigation strategy, and forum selection.

This article highlights key distinctions among the likelihood-of-confusion tests applied across the federal courts, all of which treat their factors as non-exhaustive and context-dependent.

The Polaroid and Sleekcraft Models: Similar Inputs, Different Emphases

The Second Circuit’s Polaroid test, articulated in Polaroid Corporation v. Polarad Electronics Corporation, 287 F.2d 492 (2d Cir. 1961), and the Ninth Circuit’s Sleekcraft test, from AMF Incorporated v. Sleekcraft Boats, 599 F.2d 341 (9th Cir. 1979), are the most representative frameworks for understanding likelihood-of-confusion analysis because nearly every other circuit’s formulation maps onto one of their two basic approaches. The tests are strikingly similar with respect to their relevant factors:

Polaroid factors (Second Circuit)

  1. Strength of the plaintiff’s mark
  2. Similarity of the marks
  3. Proximity of the products or services
  4. Likelihood that the prior owner will bridge the gap
  5. Evidence of actual confusion
  6. Defendant’s good or bad faith (intent)
  7. Quality of the defendant’s product or service
  8. Sophistication of the relevant consumers

Sleekcraft factors (Ninth Circuit)

  1. Strength of the mark
  2. Proximity or relatedness of the goods or services
  3. Similarity of the marks
  4. Evidence of actual confusion
  5. Marketing channels used
  6. Type of goods and the degree of care likely to be exercised by purchasers
  7. Defendant’s intent in selecting the mark
  8. Likelihood of expansion of the product lines

As indicated by the bold font, both tests examine mark strength, similarity, proximity of the goods or services, evidence of actual confusion, the defendant’s intent, and consumer care. But despite this overlap, the tests guide courts toward different kinds of evidence and analytical emphases.

Sleekcraft focuses on how consumers actually encounter marks in the marketplace. By separating marketing channels, degree of consumer care, and type of goods into distinct factors, it requires courts to analyze how confusion would occur in practice. The test maps onto the consumer’s path through the marketplace and encourages evidence about advertising platforms, sales environments, pricing, and purchasing behavior.

Polaroid, on the other hand, is more conceptual and flexible. It collapses those same components into broader questions about “proximity” and “consumer sophistication,” leaving courts more room to emphasize or downplay particular marketplace details as they see fit. As such, a court applying the Polaroid test may or may not evaluate marketing channels as part of its “proximity” or “consumer sophistication” factors, whereas a court bound by Sleekcraft would do so expressly. As a result, two courts can apply these “substantially similar” tests and still reach different results on the same facts.

The DuPont Factors: A Different Tool for a Different Job

The Federal Circuit and the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board (TTAB) apply a more comprehensive list of up to thirteen factors, known as the DuPont factors. Unlike Polaroid and Sleekcraft, DuPont expressly accounts for the broader market structure, by including factors such as the number of similar marks in use, length of use, coexistence without confusion, and any other factors probative of confusion.

This breadth makes sense in the registration context, where the Patent and Trademark Office (PTO) must consider not just the parties before it, but the integrity of the register as a whole. The drawback of the DuPont test, however, is its tendency to place less emphasis on real-world consumer perception between the relevant products, which is the core concern in infringement litigation. 

Tests in Other Circuits

The remaining circuits apply likelihood-of-confusion tests that track the same core considerations, but with distinct formulations and emphases.

  • The First Circuit applies the Pignons factors, focusing heavily on similarity, channels of trade, and evidence of actual confusion.
  • The Third Circuit’s Lapp factors are more detailed and resemble Sleekcraft in their attention to market conditions and purchaser care.
  • The Fourth Circuit uses the Pizzeria Uno factors, which tend to emphasize mark strength and similarity.
  • The Fifth Circuit’s “Digits of Confusion” test closely parallels Polaroid and often gives substantial weight to intent and actual confusion.
  • The Sixth Circuit applies the Frisch factors, which are structurally similar to Sleekcraft but often applied more holistically.
  • The Seventh Circuit favors a flexible, evidence-driven approach that downplays formal factor counting and emphasizes consumer perception.
  • The Eighth Circuit’s SquirtCo factors resemble Polaroid but frequently stress competitive proximity.
  • The Tenth Circuit applies six factors, sometimes called the “Beer Nuts factors,” which place particular emphasis on similarity and market overlap.
  • The Eleventh Circuit’s Frehling factors closely track Polaroid and Sleekcraft but often elevate mark strength and similarity.

These differences rarely change the legal standard, but they influence how courts weigh evidence.

Practical Implications

For litigants, these differences affect everything from forum selection to discovery priorities and evidentiary strategy. In a Sleekcraft jurisdiction, for example, marketing channels and purchasing context deserve early attention, whereas in courts applying Polaroid, strength and similarity evidence may dominate. In TTAB proceedings, failure to address DuPont-specific market structure factors can be fatal.

The broader takeaway is that likelihood of confusion is not a single test with local labels. It is a family of related frameworks that reward different kinds of evidence and different litigation strategies. Litigators should identify these circuit-specific differences at the outset and tailor their evidence and arguments to the governing framework.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Nike’s Total 90 Trademark Revival Runs Into Reverse Confusion Claim
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
Nike is defending its revived Total 90 soccer line against trademark infringement claims brought by a smaller company that obtained federal registration for the mark after Nike’s earlier registration lapsed. In Total90 LLC v. Nike, Inc., No. 2:25-cv-02325 (E.D. La.), filed in November 2025, the dispute centers on whether Nike’s asserted common-law rights and continued use prevail over the plaintiff’s registration. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion for temporary restraining order (TRO), but the case underscores the risks of letting legacy marks lapse, especially before major marketing campaigns.

Case Background

Nike launched its iconic Total 90 soccer cleat line in 2000 but allowed its federal registration for “TOTAL 90” to lapse in 2019 as primary use declined. In 2022, Hugh Bartlett, a New Orleans–based engineer and youth soccer coach, obtained a federal registration for the nearly identical mark “TOTAL90” through Total90 LLC, based on use beginning in 2019 for a fantasy soccer app and later expanding into apparel and footwear.

Nike began relaunching its retro Total 90 products in March 2025, seeking to capitalize on nostalgia ahead of the 2026 World Cup. In December 2024, Bartlett allegedly contacted Nike to propose a collaboration. Negotiations continued for nearly a year but soured when, according to Nike, Bartlett demanded $2.5 million to assign the registration or face litigation. Total90 LLC then filed suit in November 2025, asserting claims for trademark infringement, unfair competition, and reverse confusion under the Lanham Act. Total90 LLC sought a TRO, preliminary injunction, damages, and destruction of infringing materials.

Total90 LLC argued that Nike abandoned the “TOTAL 90” mark when its federal registration lapsed in 2019, and that Nike’s high-profile relaunch was creating reverse confusion, causing consumers to mistakenly associate Total90’s products with Nike. Nike countered by asserting its senior-user rights, emphasizing continuous use of the mark since 2000. While acknowledging the expired registration, Nike maintained that it had never discontinued use or forfeited its significant brand equity in the mark. On November 26, 2025, Chief Judge Wendy B. Vitter denied Total90’s motion for a temporary restraining order, finding insufficient evidence of likelihood of success or irreparable harm. A preliminary injunction hearing is scheduled for January 8, 2026. 

Key Takeaways for Trademark Practitioners and Brand Owners

Although the case is still pending, it already highlights key principles regarding abandonment, priority, and the limits of federal registration.

1. Abandonment Is Not Automatic Upon Lapsed Registration

A core issue in the case is whether Nike abandoned its rights to the “TOTAL 90”mark by failing to renew. Under 15 U.S.C. § 1127, a mark is abandoned when “its use has been discontinued with intent not to resume,” or when the owner “causes the mark to become the generic name for the goods or services on or in connection with which it is used or otherwise to lose its significance as a mark.” Nonuse for three consecutive years creates a rebuttable presumption of abandonment, but the burden is on the challenger to prove both discontinuance and intent. Mere failure to renew a registration does not constitute abandonment. Jean Patou, Inc. v. Aristocrat Products Corp., 202 U.S.P.Q. 130, 1979 WL 24833 (T.T.A.B. 1979). And courts routinely recognize that the lapse of a federal registration does not extinguish common-law rights where bona fide use continues. See, e.g., Crash Dummy Movie, LLC v. Mattel, Inc., 601 F.3d 1387, 1391 (Fed. Cir. 2010). As Judge Vitter noted, U.S. trademark rights flow from use, not registration. 

Here, Nike offered evidence of continued commercial use after its registration lapsed, including resale activity, limited releases, and existing inventory, which undermined any claim of abandonment. The takeaway for brand owners is that even minimal ongoing use of dormant marks should be documented to defend against abandonment claims. At the same time, while residual or secondary-market sales can preserve priority for dormant product lines, extended minimal use increases vulnerability, especially if a third party later adopts and registers the mark in good faith.

2. Senior Common-Law Rights Trump Junior Registration

Priority for marks is determined by first use in commerce rather than registration. This means a senior common-law user’s prior rights prevail over a junior party’s later federal registration, even if incontestable. The registration’s presumptions of exclusivity under § 1057(b) and § 1115(a) are rebuttable by evidence of earlier use (§ 1115(b)(5)), allowing the prior user to continue using the mark in geographic regions in which it used the mark prior to the junior party’s registration. Courts have consistently upheld this principle. See Wrist-Rocket Mfg. Co. v. Saunders Archery Co., 578 F.2d 727, 731 (8th Cir. 1978) (incontestability yields to prior common-law rights); Old Dutch Foods, Inc. v. Dan Dee Pretzel & Potato Chip Co., 477 F.2d 150, 157 (6th Cir. 1973); Burger King of Florida, Inc. v. Hoots, 403 F.2d 904, 909 (7th Cir. 1968). 

In Nike v. Total90 LLC, the court’s denial of a TRO against Nike reflects this framework, signaling that Nike’s decades of prior use (since 2000) likely establish senior common-law rights that override Total90 LLC’s 2022 registration, permitting Nike’s continued sales pending full adjudication.

3. Challenges in Proving Reverse Confusion

Total90 LLC advances a reverse confusion theory, alleging that Nike’s massive promotional efforts and market dominance in relaunching “Total 90” have overwhelmed its smaller brand, causing consumers to mistakenly believe Total90 LLC’s goods are affiliated with, or sponsored by, Nike, eroding the junior registrant’s distinct identity. Although reverse confusion is a recognized doctrine (e.g., Dreamwerks Prod. Grp., Inc. v. SKG Studio, 142 F.3d 1127, 1130 (9th Cir. 1998), it demands robust proof of likelihood of confusion, including actual evidence of consumer misassociation. In denying emergency relief, the court found Total90 LLC’s preliminary showing lacking, noting the absence of evidence of actual confusion and the use of distinct retail channels. The court also emphasized Total90 LLC’s 11-month delay in seeking a TRO after negotiations, which undercut any claim of irreparable harm under the Trademark Modernization Act.

This aspect of the dispute highlights the high evidentiary bar for reverse confusion claims, particularly where a smaller junior registrant faces a senior common-law user with substantial legacy goodwill. Success typically requires early, persuasive evidence of actual consumer confusion, such as surveys, misdirected inquiries, or anecdotal proof, coupled with prompt action to preserve any presumption of irreparable harm. Brand owners who acquire lapsed marks should begin gathering confusion evidence as soon as a legacy brand reenters the market, closely monitor overlapping channels, and avoid extended negotiations that may undermine claims of urgency. Conversely, larger brands reviving dormant marks benefit from documenting continuous, even limited, use to defeat abandonment arguments.

4. Portfolio Management and Revival Risks

Nike’s experience offers a practical lesson for brands reviving legacy marks. Even well-known names benefit from consistent portfolio maintenance, including timely § 8 and § 9 filings and routine monitoring for third-party registrations. Docketing systems and periodic audits of dormant marks help reduce the risk that nostalgic brands are picked up by opportunistic registrants.

For smaller companies, acquiring a lapsed registration can create leverage, but enforcing it against a senior user with substantial historical goodwill carries real risk, including challenges based on prior use or allegations of bad-faith adoption. As the case moves forward, discovery may shed additional light on Nike’s post-2019 use and intent, and a decision on the merits could clarify how courts distinguish token use from bona fide commercial use in brand-revival scenarios.

Conclusion

In sum, although Nike cleared the initial TRO hurdle, the dispute underscores an important principle: trademark rights are grounded in use, not registration alone. For brand owners, the case reinforces the importance of disciplined portfolio maintenance, maintaining evidence of ongoing use, and acting promptly when revived marks attract competing third-party claims.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Trademark Infringement, Dilution, and False Advertising Under the Lanham Act
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
The Lanham Act (15 U.S.C. § 1051 et seq.) is the primary federal statute governing trademarks and unfair competition. It provides protections against three distinct but related harms: trademark infringement, trademark dilution, and false advertising. All three aim to promote fair competition and protect both consumers and brand owners, but they address different types of misconduct. Trademark infringement focuses on consumer confusion, dilution protects the uniqueness of famous marks, and false advertising targets deceptive commercial statements. Understanding their elements, differences, and appropriate applications is crucial for businesses navigating brand protection.

Trademark Infringement

Trademark infringement occurs when an unauthorized party uses a mark (or a confusingly similar one) in a way that is likely to cause consumer confusion about the source, sponsorship, or affiliation of goods or services. This claim protects any valid trademark (registered or unregistered) from uses that mislead the public.

The core elements of a federal trademark infringement claim under Sections 32 (for registered marks) or 43(a) (for unregistered marks) of the Lanham Act are:

  1. The plaintiff owns a valid, protectable trademark;
  2. The plaintiff has priority (i.e., used the mark first); and
  3. The defendant’s use of a similar mark in connection with the sale of goods or services is likely to cause confusion among consumers.

See 15 U.S.C. § 1114(1)(a); 15 U.S.C. § 1125(a)(1)(A). For infringement claims, likelihood of confusion is key. This is assessed through multi-factor analyses such as the Polaroid factors (in the Second circuit) or the Sleekcraft factors (in the Ninth Circuit). Common factors, which courts weigh holistically, include strength of the plaintiff’s mark, degree of similarity between the marks, similarity of the goods or services, evidence of actual confusion, marketing channels, defendant’s intent, and buyer sophistication.

Remedies for infringement include injunctions, damages (actual or profits), attorney’s fees in exceptional cases, and destruction of infringing goods.

Trademark Dilution

Dilution protects famous trademarks from uses that weaken their distinctiveness or harm their reputation, even absent confusion or competition. Enacted by the Federal Trademark Dilution Act (1995) and revised by the Trademark Dilution Revision Act (2006) under Section 43(c) of the Lanham Act, dilution targets the whittling away of a mark’s selling power. 

Elements for a federal dilution claim include:

  1. The plaintiff’s mark is famous (i.e., is widely recognized by the general public, determined by factors like duration/extent of use, advertising, sales volume, and actual recognition);
  2. The defendant uses a similar mark in commerce;
  3. The defendant’s use began after the plaintiff’s mark became famous; and
  4. The use is likely to cause dilution.

Jada Toys, Inc. v. Mattel, Inc., 518 F.3d 628, 634 (9th Cir. 2008). Dilution comes in two forms: dilution by blurring, and dilution by tarnishment. 15 U.S.C. § 1125(c)(1). Blurring has to do with an association that weakens the mark’s distinctiveness. This occurs when the similar mark reduces the public’s immediate connection between the famous mark and its unique source. In analyzing claims for blurring, courts consider factors like similarity, distinctiveness of the famous mark, exclusivity of use, degree of recognition, intent to associate, and actual association. Classic examples include “Buick aspirin” or “Kodak pianos.” See Mead Data Cent., Inc. v. Toyota Motor Sales, U.S.A., Inc., 875 F.2d 1026, 1031 (2d Cir. 1989). Although these are unlikely to confuse consumers about the source, the famous mark’s distinctiveness is diminished.

Dilution by tarnishment, on the other hand, is about an association that harms the famous mark’s reputation. This may occur by linking the famous mark to inferior or unsavory products. For example, the Second Circuit held that a defendant’s poster reading “Enjoy Cocaine,” styled in lettering and colors similar to Coca-Cola’s iconic “Enjoy Coca-Cola” mark, could tarnish Coca-Cola and harm its business reputation. Coca-Cola Co. v. Gemini Rising, Inc., 346 F. Supp. 1183, 1191-92 (E.D.N.Y. 1972).

Unlike infringement, proof of confusion, competition, or actual economic injury is unnecessary for dilution claims. The likelihood of dilution alone is sufficient to obtain remedies, which primarily include injunctions. Monetary damages can also be awarded if the defendant willfully intended to trade on the owner’s reputation or to cause dilution. 15 U.S.C. § 1125(c)(5).

False Advertising

False advertising, under Section 43(a)(1)(B) of the Lanham Act, prohibits misleading statements in commercial advertising or promotion. It allows competitors (or others with standing) to challenge deceptive claims that harm their interests.

Key elements of false advertising include:

  1. The defendant made a false or misleading statement of fact in commercial advertising or promotion;
  2. The statement actually deceives or has a tendency to deceive a substantial portion of the audience (literal falsity needs no extrinsic evidence; implied falsity often requires consumer surveys);
  3. The deception is material (i.e., likely to influence purchasing decisions);
  4. The advertised goods or services traveled in interstate commerce; and
  5. The plaintiff has been or is likely to be injured (e.g., lost sales or reputational harm).

Southland Sod Farms v. Stover Seed Co., 108 F.3d 1134, 1139 (9th Cir. 1997). Standing requires the plaintiff to be within the “zone of interest” by establishing commercial injury such as lost sales or damage to business reputation. Lexmark Intern., Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc., 572 U.S. 118 (2014). Remedies are similar to those for infringement, including injunctions, damages, and potentially the cost of corrective advertising. See, e.g., Larry Pitt & Assoc. v. Lundy Law LLP, 294 F.Supp.3d 329 (2018).

Choosing the Right Claims

Trademark infringement, dilution, and false advertising differ significantly in scope and requirements. Understanding those differences helps clarify when each claim is most appropriate.

Infringement protects any valid trademark, famous or not, and focuses on the likelihood of consumer confusion as the primary harm. It typically arises in competitive or related markets where similar marks on similar goods or services could mislead buyers about origin or affiliation. In practice, infringement is the go-to for most trademark disputes because it directly addresses market diversion and allows successful plaintiffs to recover damages or lost profits.

Dilution, by contrast, applies only to famous marks and protects against harm to a mark’s distinctiveness or reputation, regardless of consumer confusion or direct competition. Unlike infringement or false advertising, where competition is often relevant, dilution can occur even between entirely unrelated products, because the focus is on either blurring the mark’s uniqueness or tarnishing its image. Dilution claims are particularly suitable when the mark’s prestige or uniqueness is threatened without any likelihood of confusion. For example, in Nike, Incorporated v. Nikepal International, Incorporated, Nike successfully proved dilution by blurring based on Nikepal’s use of “NIKEPAL” for laboratory products and services completely unrelated to apparel. 2007 WL 2782030 (E.D. Cal. Sept. 18, 2007). The court emphasized the similar identity of the marks, the extraordinary fame and distinctiveness of NIKE, and survey evidence showing an 87% association rate among relevant consumers, and entered a permanent injunction barring use of the NIKEPAL mark. Id. at *8. 

False advertising stands apart because it does not require any trademark involvement. It targets deceptive statements in promotion, where the primary harm is consumer deception that influences purchasing decisions rather than confusion about source. While infringement and dilution both involve mark usage, false advertising can involve purely factual misrepresentations (literal or implied) completely unrelated to branding. Thus, false advertising is used for deceptive claims without mark misuse (e.g., a competitor falsely claiming “clinically proven” superiority). 

The distinctions among infringement, dilution, and false advertising shape both litigation strategy and risk assessment. Each claim targets a different type of competitive harm, and misalignment between theory and facts can weaken even strong brands’ positions. Evaluating these claims early, with careful attention to evidence and jurisdiction, helps brand owners safeguard their marks and make informed strategic choices.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Corporate Boundaries in Trademark Profit Awards: Dewberry Group, Inc. v. Dewberry Engineers, Inc.
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
The U.S. Supreme Court’s unanimous February 26, 2025, decision in Dewberry Group, Inc. v. Dewberry Engineers, Inc., 604 U.S. 321 (2025), marked a significant reaffirmation of corporate separateness in trademark infringement remedies. Authored by Justice Kagan, the Court held that profit disgorgement under 15 U.S.C. § 1117(a) is limited to the named defendant’s earnings, absent veil-piercing. This ruling reinforces limits on equitable relief and highlights strategic pleading for trademark plaintiffs. As of January 2026, the case remains pending on remand to the Fourth Circuit.

Case Background

The dispute stemmed from a trademark dispute between two unrelated real estate companies sharing the “Dewberry” name. Dewberry Engineers, a long-established engineering firm, holds a registered trademark in “Dewberry” for real estate services. In 2007, it sued Dewberry Group, a real estate development company owned by John Dewberry, for infringement, leading to a settlement that restricted Dewberry Group’s use of the name.

By 2020, Dewberry Group violated the agreement through a rebranding effort, creating brands like Dewberry Living, Dewberry Office, and Studio Dewberry. Dewberry Engineers filed suit in the Eastern District of Virginia, claiming Dewberry Group’s rebranding breached the agreement and infringed under the Lanham Act (15 U.S.C. § 1117(a)). Dewberry Group provides services to non-party affiliates that own commercial rental properties, and earns only modest fees while the affiliates record the rental profits. This structure became central to the damages phase.

Procedural History

The Eastern District of Virginia found Dewberry Group liable for willful infringement. At a bench trial, the court awarded $43 million in disgorged profits, treating Dewberry Group and its affiliates as a “single entity” to capture the “economic reality” of their operations.

In a split decision, the Fourth Circuit affirmed, reasoning that ignoring corporate formalities prevented evasion of liability. The dissent argued disgorgement should be limited to the defendant’s profits absent veil-piercing.

Parties’ Arguments on Appeal to the Supreme Court

Dewberry Group argued that the Lanham Act’s text limits disgorgement to the “defendant’s profits,” meaning only the named defendant’s earnings—here, Dewberry Group's modest fees, not the affiliates' rental income. They contended that treating affiliates as part of the defendant ignored corporate separateness, a bedrock principle of U.S. law, absent veil-piercing (which Dewberry Engineers did not pursue). Dewberry Group emphasized that Congress did not intend for courts to routinely aggregate affiliate profits without explicit statutory authority, warning that doing so would undermine predictable business planning.

Dewberry Engineers argued that the Lanham Act’s equitable remedies allows courts to consider the “economic reality” of an infringer’s operations to prevent infringers from evading liability through affiliates. Specifically, Dewberry Engineers highlighted the “just-sum provision” (§ 1117(a)), which states that courts may exercise their discretion and adjust the award to a “just” amount if the amount of recovery based on profits is “inadequate or excessive.” Dewberry Engineers argued that this provision permits adjustments for inadequate or excessive awards, including incorporating affiliate profits to reflect the defendant’s “true financial gain.” Dewberry Engineers portrayed Dewberry Group’s structure as manipulative, with profits shifted to affiliates to shield infringement proceeds, and urged the Court to uphold the lower courts’ flexible approach to ensure the Act’s deterrent purpose.

Supreme Court Ruling

The Supreme Court vacated the award, holding that “defendant’s profits,” as used in § 1117(a) of the Lanham Act, means only the named defendant’s earnings. The Court reasoned that “defendant” carries its ordinary meaning—the sued party—and federal statutes incorporate traditional corporate law principles treating affiliates as distinct unless veil-pierced for fraud or similar abuses. Absent such a showing (which Dewberry Engineers conceded it did not make), aggregating profits violates this rule. The opinion rejected the lower courts’ “single entity” approach as unsupported by the Act’s text, which targets unjust enrichment without authorizing routine disregard of formalities. The Court stated, in no uncertain terms, that “the ‘defendant’s profits’ are the defendant’s profits, not its plus its affiliates’.” Dewberry Group, Inc. v. Dewberry Engineers Inc., 604 U.S. 321, 327 (2025).

The Court also rejected Dewberry Engineers’s reliance on the “just-sum provision,” noting that the lower courts did not invoke it. Those courts simply aggregated all of the Dewberry entities’ real-estate profits for the relevant years, rather than identifying which of the affiliates’ profits were properly attributable to Dewberry Group, and adjusting the award to an amount reflecting the Dewberry Group’s own gain. In other words, the lower courts just redefined “defendant” by treating Dewberry Group and its affiliates as a single corporate entity. In doing so, the lower courts “approved an award including non-defendants’ profits—and thus went further than the Lanham Act permits.” Dewberry Group, 604 U.S. at 329. 

The Court vacated the judgment and remanded the case for a new award proceeding. In doing so, the Court left open the question of whether courts can use the “just-sum” provision to support affiliate-based awards or scrutinize  accounting to assess “true financial gain.” The remand to the Fourth Circuit remains pending, as of January 2026.

Justice Sotomayor's Concurrence

Justice Sotomayor joined the opinion fully but concurred separately to stress that corporate separateness does not require ignoring “economic realities” or “clever accounting” in calculating the named defendant’s profits. Given Dewberry Group’s reported losses versus affiliates’ substantial infringing gains, she noted potential remand tools, like scrutinizing books, attributing below-market fees, or using the “just-sum” provision, to reflect true financial benefit without aggregating non-party profits.

Implications for Businesses and Employers

This decision provides employers and businesses with greater certainty in structuring operations through affiliates to limit liability exposure. By maintaining separate entities, companies can limit disgorgement exposure, as long as they maintain formalities and grounds for veil-piercing do not exist. For example, parent companies might use subsidiaries for high-risk activities, ensuring infringement claims target only the infringing entity’s assets.

However, the ruling does not immunize affiliates from liability if properly named as defendants. Plaintiffs may now more aggressively amend complaints or pursue veil-piercing arguments, especially where a parent company is undercapitalized or has commingled funds. 

For trademark owners, the case underscores the importance of thorough pre-suit investigation to identify all potentially liable entities. In-house counsel should consider joint liability theories early and weigh the costs of broader litigation.

Overall, the ruling reinforces equitable principles in trademark remedies while preserving corporate shields, potentially influencing how employers design group structures for IP-sensitive operations. In light of this development, businesses should consult counsel to review affiliate arrangements.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Ninth Circuit Affirms Dismissal in Hara v. Netflix: Balancing Trademarks and Artistic Freedom
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals recently affirmed the dismissal of a Lanham Act lawsuit brought by drag performer Vicky Vox against Netflix and related parties. In Hara v. Netflix, Inc. (No. 23-3768, decided July 28, 2025), the court clarified the application of key legal tests in trademark disputes involving expressive media. The decision provides important guidance for those in creative fields like film or television, as it offers insights into navigating the boundary between brand protection and artistic expression in the wake of the Supreme Court’s decision in Jack Daniel’s Properties, Inc. v. VIP Products LLC.

The Legal Framework: Rogers, Jack Daniel’s, and Source Identification

The Lanham Act protects against consumer confusion regarding the origin, affiliation, or endorsement of goods or services. For celebrities, that protection can extend to unauthorized uses of their name, image, or likeness that imply sponsorship, which are typically analyzed under the likelihood-of-confusion test. See Twentieth Century Fox Television v. Empire Distrib., Inc., 875 F.3d 1192, 1196 (9th Cir. 2017).

When the challenged use appears in an expressive work, however, the Ninth Circuit applies the test articulated in Rogers v. Grimaldi, 875 F.2d 994 (2d Cir. 1989). Under Rogers, the Lanham Act does not apply unless the use of the mark has no artistic relevance to the underlying work or explicitly misleads consumers as to the source or origin of the work.

The continued viability of Rogers was called into question by the Supreme Court’s 2023 decision in Jack Daniel’s. There, the Court held that Rogers does not apply when a defendant uses a mark as a source identifier for its own goods, as was the case with a parodic dog toy designed to mimic a Jack Daniel’s whiskey bottle. In cases decided after Jack Daniel’s, including Punchbowl, Inc. v. AJ Press, LLC, 90 F.4th 1022 (9th Cir. 2024), the Ninth Circuit clarified that Rogers remains applicable where a mark is used solely for expressive purposes and not to designate the source of the infringer’s goods. In other words, Rogers is inapplicable only when the alleged infringer uses the mark to identify the source of its own product.

Against that backdrop, the central threshold inquiry in Hara was whether the challenged depiction functioned as a source identifier or merely as part of the show’s expressive content.

The Dispute: Vicky Vox and Q-Force

Drag performer Lance Hara, professionally known as Vicky Vox, sued Netflix over its 2021 animated series Q-Force, a satirical show about underappreciated LGBTQ+ spies. Vox alleged that her likeness was used without authorization in a ten-second West Hollywood bar scene, a teaser trailer, and a promotional image. According to the complaint, a background character with red-orange hair, cat-eye makeup, and a fan “thworp” resembled her.

Vox asserted claims for false endorsement under the Lanham Act, citing inquiries from members of the public about a possible association with the show, as well as state-law right-of-publicity claims. The district court dismissed the federal claims on First Amendment grounds and declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the state claims. Vox appealed.

The Ninth Circuit’s Holding: RogersTest Bars the Claims

The Ninth Circuit concluded that Vox’s claims were governed by the Rogers test. The court reasoned that brief depiction of Vox’s likeness did not identify the source or origin of Q-Force. Instead, it functioned as part of the show’s expressive content. Because the likeness was not used as a trademark or source identifier, Rogers, rather than the likelihood-of-confusion test, applied.

Applying Rogers, the panel held that Vox failed to satisfy either prong. As to artistic relevance, the depiction easily cleared the low “above zero” threshold. The animated drag queen contributed meaningfully to the show’s setting, humor, and cultural commentary, particularly in a scene set in a West Hollywood bar. Turning to the second prong, the court found no overt claims or explicit misrepresentations suggesting that Vox endorsed or was affiliated with the show. Evidence of consumer confusion, including inquiries about a possible connection, was deemed irrelevant because the Rogers analysis focuses on the defendant’s conduct, not the downstream effects of the use.

Because the promotional materials were integral to the expressive work itself, the panel unanimously held that the district court properly dismissed Vox’s Lanham Act claims. 

Guidance for Future Cases

The opinion provides important post-Jack Daniel’s clarification. In the Ninth Circuit, Rogers remains a “shortcut to dismissal” for non–source-identifying uses in expressive works, even when the use suggests affiliation. The central inquiry is whether the mark functions to identify the work’s source or origin. If the mark is instead used solely for other expressive purposes (such as realism, commentary, or satire) Rogers applies, and evidence of consumer confusion or intent will not defeat dismissal absent an explicitly misleading use. This framework draws a clearer line between artistic depiction and commercial branding, offering a more predictable roadmap for evaluating future claims.

Implications for Creative Industries

For creators in animation, film, and television, Hara is a significant development because it confirms that brief, incidental uses of real-life figures for authenticity are generally protected from Lanham Act claims. The decision encourages the use of cultural references to enrich storytelling, provided those references do not function as source identifiers or make explicit claims of endorsement. Satirical and themed works like Q-Force are likely to benefit from this framework, but more prominent or dominant depictions may still warrant closer scrutiny.

At the same time, the ruling does not eliminate all risk. Producers should consult counsel early in development, especially for projects involving celebrities, to assess potential exposure. Overall, the decision reinforces a strong commitment to free expression by favoring protected speech over speculative claims of consumer confusion.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: Lessons From the New York Jets’ Battle Over “Gotham City Football”
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
The New York Jets’ recent adoption of the phrase “Gotham City Football” through the NFL’s “Rivalries” alternate uniform program has triggered a familiar trademark problem: when does a branding phrase tied to a location become protectable?

In June 2025, the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) issued a final refusal of the Jets’ trademark application. The USPTO grounded its decision in two bedrock principles of trademark law: (1) geographic descriptiveness under Section 2(e)(2) of the Lanham Act, and (2) likelihood of confusion under Section 2(d). The case offers a clear illustration of the limits of trademark protection for location-inspired branding, even when layered with fictional themes.

Unlike almost every defense in the NFL this season, the USPTO’s final refusal did not stop the Jets from moving forward. They debuted their dark, stylized “Gotham City Football” uniforms on December 7, 2025, against the Miami Dolphins.
