Once an application has been approved by an examining attorney (whether after initial review or following responses to office actions) it advances to publication. This stage opens the application to potential challenges from third parties. Many applications advance from publication to registration without issue, but understanding the opposition process and post-publication requirements is essential to protecting the mark and avoiding delays. 

This article explores the path from publication to allowance or registration and highlights the key procedural and strategic considerations at each stage.

Approval for Publication in the Official Gazette

After the examining attorney determines that an application meets all statutory and procedural requirements, the USPTO approves the mark for publication. This approval typically occurs after any office actions are resolved, but timelines can vary based on USPTO workload and the application’s complexity.

The mark is then published in the Trademark Official Gazette (TMOG), a weekly online publication issued by the USPTO. Publication serves as public notice that the USPTO intends to register the mark, giving interested parties an opportunity to potentially challenge it. Publication, which is approximately one month after approval, initiates a 30-day opposition period, during which any person or entity believing they would be damaged by the registration may file a notice of opposition. If no opposition is filed, the application proceeds toward registration, though it may take an additional three to four months to receive official notification of the next steps.

The Opposition Period

The 30-day opposition window provides an opportunity for third parties to contest registration. Potential opposers must file a notice of opposition electronically through the USPTO’s Electronic System for Trademark Trials and Appeals (ESTTA), paying the required fee (which is currently $600 per class). The notice must specify grounds for opposition, such as likelihood of confusion with an existing mark, descriptiveness, or lack of distinctiveness.

Oppositions are relatively uncommon. USPTO data indicate that approximately 7,000 to 8,000 oppositions are filed annually, representing less than 2% of the hundreds of thousands of marks published each year. This low rate reflects the rigorous examination process that weeds out many conflicts earlier, but it underscores the importance of thorough pre-filing clearance searches to minimize risks.

Potential opposers may also request extensions of time to oppose, initially for 30 days (no good cause or consent required) or 90 days (with good cause shown, no consent required), to investigate or negotiate. If an opposition is filed, the process shifts to the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board (TTAB) for resolution.

Handling Oppositions and TTAB Proceedings

An opposition proceeding is an inter partes, adversarial process conducted before the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board (TTAB), an administrative tribunal within the USPTO. Although it shares structural similarities with federal court litigation, the process is generally more streamlined and cost-effective, relying primarily on written submissions, limited discovery, and briefing rather than live testimony or a full trial, unless specifically requested.

The opposer, acting as the plaintiff, must establish standing and prove valid grounds for refusal, while the applicant defends the registrability of the mark. Common grounds for opposition include priority of use, likelihood of confusion, and claims that a mark is generic or merely descriptive. Proceedings typically unfold in defined phases, including discovery, the submission of evidence during the trial period, and written briefing. Oral hearings are optional and relatively uncommon.

In practice, most oppositions resolve through settlement before a final decision is issued, often through coexistence agreements or amendments to the identification of goods or services. If the TTAB ultimately rules in favor of the opposer, the application may be refused in whole or in part, subject to the applicant’s right to appeal the decision to federal court.

Applicants facing opposition should consult a U.S.-licensed trademark attorney with experience in TTAB proceedings. While early settlements may resolve disputes for several thousand dollars, fully litigated oppositions can exceed $75,000. Strategic early resolution can significantly reduce costs. During an opposition, limited amendments to the application may be permitted with TTAB approval, though such changes may require republication of the mark.

Notice of Allowance for Intent-to-Use Applications

The path after publication diverges based on the application’s filing basis. For use-based applications (filed under Section 1(a) of the Lanham Act), if no opposition is filed or it is unsuccessful, the mark proceeds directly to registration. The USPTO issues the registration certificate shortly thereafter.

In contrast, intent-to-use (ITU) applications (filed under Section 1(b), based on a bona fide intent to use the mark) receive a Notice of Allowance (NOA) after the opposition period ends without challenge or upon resolution of any opposition. The NOA indicates that the mark is allowable but not yet registered, as actual use in commerce must be demonstrated.

Foreign-based applications (under Section 44 or 66) follow a similar path to ITU, requiring proof of use or excusable nonuse in some cases.

Filing Statement of Use or Extensions

For ITU applications, the applicant must file a Statement of Use (SOU) within six months of the NOA issuance date, submitting specimens showing the mark in actual use in commerce for the specified goods or services. The SOU must be filed via TEAS, accompanied by a fee ($150 per class) and verified under penalty of perjury. 

If use has not commenced, applicants may request extensions of time, each for an additional six months, up to five extensions total (for a maximum of 36 months from the NOA date). Each extension requires a fee of $125 per class (if filed electronically) and a statement affirming continued bona fide intent to use the mark. Extensions are not automatic and must demonstrate good cause after the first one, such as ongoing product development or market research.

Failure to file a timely SOU or extension results in abandonment. Once an SOU is accepted, the mark proceeds to registration, provided no further issues arise.

Issuance of Registration Certificate

Upon successful completion of all requirements (including surviving publication and opposition, and for ITU applications, acceptance of the SOU) the USPTO issues a registration certificate. This grants nationwide constructive notice of ownership, prima facie evidence of validity, and enhanced remedies against infringement.

Registration typically occurs three to four months after the end of the opposition period for use-based applications, or after SOU acceptance for ITU. Applicants receive the certificate electronically or by mail, and the mark is entered on the Principal Register (or Supplemental Register, if applicable).

Key Considerations During and After the Opposition Period

Several strategic factors can influence success during the publication and opposition phase:

  1. Monitoring for Oppositions: Applicants should actively monitor the Trademark Official Gazette for their publication and consider trademark watching services to identify potential opposers early. In some cases, proactive outreach can resolve concerns before a formal opposition is filed.
  1. Deadlines for SOU and Extensions: For ITU applicants, the initial six-month SOU window is strict, with extensions providing flexibility up to three years total. Careful deadline tracking is essential, as missed deadlines generally result in abandonment with no meaningful opportunity for revival.
  1. Limited Post-NOA Amendments: After issuance of the NOA (or post-publication for use-based), amendments are restricted. Broadening goods/services or changing the mark is generally prohibited. Narrowing or clarifications may be allowed, but it may require republication and restarting the opposition period.
  1. Risks of Third-Party Challenges: Beyond opposition, registered marks remain vulnerable to cancellation proceedings at the TTAB for up to five years (or indefinitely for certain grounds like fraud). Pre-filing clearance and ongoing monitoring help mitigate these risks.
  1. Differences for Use-Based vs. Intent-to-Use: Use-based applications offer quicker registration but require upfront proof of use, limiting flexibility for new brands. ITU provides time to develop use but adds SOU requirements and extension fees, with the risk of abandonment if use never materializes. The appropriate filing type depends on business timing: intent-to-use applications work well for planned launches, while use-based applications are better suited for marks already in commercial use.

Conclusion

The publication and opposition phases represent the final hurdles to trademark registration, where public input and proof of use ensure only eligible marks receive federal protection. Although oppositions are uncommon, being prepared is essential to defending an application. By understanding timelines, filing-basis distinctions, and strategic options, applicants can navigate these stages efficiently and maximize the benefits of registration. Treating potential challenges as a normal part of the process helps position a brand for long-term success.

This content is intended for general information only, not legal advice. Every legal situation is fact-specific, and you should consult qualified counsel regarding your particular situation.

item.title: USPTO Trademark Registration Series: Part 6 – Maintaining and Enforcing Your Trademark Registration
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
Obtaining a federal trademark registration from the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) is a meaningful milestone, but it is not the finish line. Registration creates powerful legal advantages only if the mark is continuously used and actively protected. Failure to maintain or enforce a registration can result in cancellation, erosion of rights, and the loss of benefits such as nationwide priority and enhanced enforcement remedies. This article outlines the mandatory and optional post-registration filings, enforcement strategies, and practical safeguards that help ensure a trademark remains a durable business asset.

Required Post-Registration Filings

To keep a registration active, owners must file specific documents with the USPTO at set intervals. These filings are mandatory, fee-based, and generally must be submitted electronically through TEAS. Registrants domiciled outside the United States must be represented by a U.S.-licensed attorney.

Declaration of Continued Use or Excusable Nonuse Under Section 8 or 71

The Declaration (or Affidavit) of Continued Use or Excusable Nonuse confirms that the mark remains in use in commerce or that any temporary nonuse is legally excusable, preventing cancellation for abandonment. Most trademark registrations rely on Section 8 of the Lanham Act (15 U.S.C. § 1058) for filing this declaration, which applies to standard U.S. federal trademark registrations. In contrast, Section 71 (15 U.S.C. § 1141k) is used specifically for international trademark registrations that have been extended to the U.S. under the Madrid Protocol.

Filings are due within one year before the end of:

  • The sixth year after registration.
  • The tenth year after registration and every ten years thereafter.

A six-month grace period is available for an additional fee. Missing the deadline (including the grace period) results in automatic cancellation (though common law rights remain).

The declaration must:

  • Identify the registration by number.
  • Be filed by the current owner.
  • Include a verified statement attesting to use (or excusable nonuse) in commerce during the period.
  • Specify covered goods or services; delete or claim excusable nonuse for those not in use to avoid deletion.
  • Contain a warning against false statements under 18 U.S.C. § 1001.

Unless excusable nonuse is claimed for all goods or services in a class, the owner must submit at least one specimen per class showing actual use in commerce. The specimen need not be visually identical to the registered mark, but it must create the same commercial impression.

Excusable nonuse is narrowly construed. It applies only where use has been temporarily discontinued due to circumstances beyond the owner’s control, coupled with a bona fide intent to resume use. Examples may include interruptions caused by natural disasters, trade embargoes, government restrictions, supply chain shutdowns, or serious illness of an indispensable owner. Nonuse driven by ordinary business decisions, rebranding, marketing strategy shifts, lack of demand, or use of the mark on different goods or services does not qualify. When excusable nonuse is claimed, the owner must explain when use stopped, why it stopped, what steps are being taken to resume use, and when use is expected to restart. Conclusory or unsupported statements are insufficient and can jeopardize the registration.

Because Section 8 and 71 declarations are verified statements of use, they are subject to the USPTO’s post-registration audit program. Registrations with broad identifications or questionable specimens may be audited, requiring additional proof of use. Failure to respond or provide adequate evidence can result in deletion of goods or cancellation of the registration.

Renewal Application (Section 9)

Trademark registrations must be renewed every ten years. A Section 9 renewal is filed during the final year of each ten-year term, with a six-month grace period available for an added fee. By contrast, international registrations renew through the International Bureau rather than the USPTO.

In practice, the Section 9 renewal is typically filed together with the required Section 8 or 71 declaration due at the same time.

Optional Post-Registration Measures

Declaration of Incontestability (Section 15)

After five consecutive years of continuous use following registration, an owner may file a Section 15 Declaration to claim incontestable status, which limits certain validity challenges, including claims that the mark is merely descriptive. The declaration must state that: (1) the mark has been in continuous use for five years, and (2) there have been no final adverse decisions or pending proceedings affecting the registration. Although the USPTO does not substantively examine Section 15 filings, incontestability can be tested in enforcement or cancellation proceedings.

Corrections, Amendments, and Ownership Updates

Errors in a registration should be corrected promptly. The USPTO will correct its own errors without charge and owner errors for a fee if made in good faith and without materially altering the registration. Material changes generally require cancellation and refiling.

Owners may either delete unused goods or services or make limited, non-material amendments, but they cannot expand the scope of the registration or alter the mark’s commercial impression.

Assignments should be recorded with the USPTO to ensure accurate ownership records and avoid complications during enforcement or renewal.

Monitoring and Enforcing Your Trademark

A federal registration does not prevent infringement on its own; owners must actively enforce their rights to preserve the mark’s distinctiveness and value. Failure to address unauthorized use, such as infringement, dilution, or generic misuse, can weaken the mark, expose the owner to liability, or limit future legal remedies. Allowing uncontrolled use, including licensing without quality controls, can lead to partial abandonment or diminished enforceability. Consistent policing deters potential infringers and strengthens rights over time, supporting claims of acquired distinctiveness or fame in legal proceedings.

Effective monitoring combines both external and internal measures. Professional watch services can track new USPTO applications, domain registrations, social media activity, online marketplaces, and other potential sources of confusion. Internally, employees should be encouraged to promptly report suspected misuse, including sound-alike marks, lookalike designs, or generic references to the mark (e.g., using it as a verb or noun). Unchecked misuse may eventually render a mark generic or create defenses like acquiescence in court.

When potential infringement is identified, consult qualified counsel to evaluate options. Responses may include sending cease-and-desist letters, filing oppositions or cancellation proceedings at the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board (TTAB), or pursuing federal litigation to secure injunctions, monetary damages, and, in exceptional cases, attorneys’ fees and costs.

Owners should always carefully manage licensing or third-party use of the mark. For instance, agreements should include approval processes, quality control obligations, and termination rights to preserve the mark’s integrity and avoid jeopardizing its enforceability.

Maintaining comprehensive records is also essential for enforcement and demonstrating the mark’s ongoing value. This includes specimens of product packaging and advertising, sales data, market research on consumer recognition, and evidence of promotional activities. Such documentation supports priority and continuous use claims in adversarial proceedings and USPTO maintenance filings, helping defend against cancellation for abandonment or nonuse.

By treating monitoring and enforcement as ongoing responsibilities rather than occasional tasks, owners ensure their trademarks remain strong, distinctive, and valuable business assets.

International Protection Through the Madrid Protocol

For businesses operating internationally, the Madrid Protocol allows U.S. registrants to extend protection to multiple member countries through a single filing. These extensions remain dependent on the U.S. registration for the first five years, making domestic maintenance especially important.

Practical Safeguards

Several administrative practices can reduce compliance risk over time:

  • Calendar and docket all deadlines to avoid accidental cancellation
  • Maintain use evidence for each listed good or service
  • Expect audits if identifications are broad or specimens appear questionable
  • Use USPTO email reminders and deposit accounts to reduce administrative risk

Conclusion

Trademark protection does not end at registration. Ongoing use, accurate filings, and active enforcement are essential to preserving rights and avoiding cancellation. Owners who treat post-registration maintenance as a core compliance function, rather than an afterthought, place their brands in a far stronger position over the long term.

This content is for general informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Trademark matters are fact-specific, and consultation with qualified counsel is recommended for individual circumstances.

item.title: Rule 40 of the Olympic Charter: An Exception to Ordinary Trademark Law
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
Rule 40 of the Olympic Charter establishes a distinct regulatory framework governing advertising and promotional activity involving Olympic participants. It restricts the commercial use of athletes’ names, images, likenesses, and sporting performances during the defined “Games period,” which begins shortly before the Opening Ceremony and ends shortly after the Closing Ceremony.

The rule is designed to preserve the exclusivity purchased by official Olympic sponsors and to protect the revenue structure that finances the Olympic Movement. In doing so, it blends trademark principles, contractual commitments imposed on participants, and host-country legal protections. However, it diverges from traditional Lanham Act analysis and conventional trademark doctrine in important ways.

Scope and Operation of Rule 40

Under Rule 40, competitors, team officials, and other team personnel participating in the Olympic Games may permit the use of their person, name, picture, or sports performances for advertising purposes only in accordance with principles established by the International Olympic Committee (IOC) Executive Board. These principles, which have been refined and reissued for successive Games, impose the following core restrictions during the Games period:

  1. Non-sponsor entities may not use Olympic intellectual property, including the Olympic rings, emblems, or protected terminology;
  2. Advertising and promotional activities may not create a direct or indirect association with the Olympic Games;
  3. Campaigns featuring participants must be “generic,” avoiding any reference to Olympic participation or performance; and
  4. In most jurisdictions, campaigns must have launched before the Games period and may not be materially intensified during that time.

Statutory Foundation: Enhanced Rights Recognized by the Supreme Court

The legal foundation for Rule 40’s expansive protections lies in the Amateur Sports Act of 1978, now known as the Ted Stevens Olympic and Amateur Sports Act (the “Act”), as interpreted by the Supreme Court in San Francisco Arts & Athletics, Inc. v. United States Olympic Committee, 483 U.S. 522 (1987). In that case, the Court upheld Congress’s grant of exclusive rights in the word “Olympic” and related symbols to the U.S. Olympic Committee.

The Court confirmed that these rights extend beyond traditional trademark protection. Unlike a standard Lanham Act claim, enforcement under the Act does not require proof of likelihood of confusion. Instead, the Court recognized that Congress granted a broader form of statutory exclusivity that allows the Olympic Committee to prohibit advertising that creates an unauthorized association with the Olympic Games, even if consumers are not confused about source or sponsorship.

For example, a company may lawfully state that it supports a particular athlete. But if its advertising links that support to the timing or location of the Games, such as by referencing “this summer in Paris” or similar cues, it will generally violate Rule 40. The violation stems not from confusion, but from the creation of an impermissible associative link.

The Court went further, however, holding that an unauthorized user of marks protected under the Act may not invoke the ordinary statutory defenses typically available in trademark litigation. Olympic-related marks therefore occupy a uniquely protected position in U.S. advertising law, reflecting Congress’s deliberate choice to safeguard the commercial value of the Games through heightened exclusivity.

Enforcement Mechanisms Under Rule 40

Rule 40 is enforced through a combination of contractual control, institutional oversight, and statutory backing. Unlike typical trademark disputes, enforcement does not depend primarily on private litigation between competitors. Instead, compliance is embedded in the Olympic regulatory structure itself.

First, athletes are contractually bound to comply with Rule 40 as a condition of eligibility. Entry forms, athlete agreements, and National Olympic Committee rules incorporate the Charter by reference. Violations may result in disciplinary measures, including withdrawal of accreditation, disqualification, or other sanctions imposed by the relevant National Olympic Committee (NOC) or the IOC.

Second, the NOCs actively monitor advertising and social media during the Games period. Campaigns that risk creating unauthorized association are often addressed through cease-and-desist communications or demands for modification. In multi-jurisdictional campaigns, coordination may occur between the NOC and the IOC to ensure consistent enforcement.

Third, Rule 40 operates alongside statutory protections in host countries and domestic legislation, such as the Act. Where necessary, these statutes may provide a basis for injunctive relief or other judicial remedies.

Taken together, these mechanisms create a layered enforcement model which includes contractual discipline for participants, regulatory oversight during the Games period, and statutory remedies when formal legal action is required.

Implications for Legal Practice

Counsel advising Olympic athletes, their representatives, non-sponsor brands, and advertising agencies must approach Rule 40 differently from routine trademark clearance. The mere absence of Olympic rings or the word “Olympic” in creative materials does not end the analysis. Instead, counsel must evaluate whether the timing, messaging, or overall context of a campaign could create an impermissible association with the Games during the protected period.

Endorsement and sponsorship agreements involving Olympic participants should therefore expressly address:

  • Clear delineation of the applicable Games period;
  • Restrictions on messaging and required pre-approval processes;
  • Social-media controls, including posting guidelines and monitoring obligations;
  • Notification and pre-clearance procedures with the relevant National Olympic Committee and the IOC (where multi-territory campaigns are involved); and
  • Representations, warranties, and indemnity provisions concerning Rule 40 compliance.

Absent these safeguards, parties risk more than a standard trademark dispute. Potential consequences include athlete discipline, contractual exposure, and injunctive relief.

Conclusion

Rule 40 reflects a deliberate departure from traditional trademark doctrine in service of a distinct commercial objective. By prohibiting unauthorized association rather than requiring proof of confusion, and by combining statutory rights and contractual enforcement, the Olympic Movement has established a distinct exclusivity framework to protect the revenue model that funds the Games.

For practitioners in trademark, advertising, and sports law, this framework underscores that the legal environment governing global sporting events may not map neatly onto traditional intellectual property principles. Effective counseling requires attention not only to trademark doctrine, but also to the specific governance and enforcement structure that shapes how these rights function in practice.

This content is for general informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Trademark matters are fact-specific, and consultation with qualified counsel is recommended for individual circumstances.

item.title: Junk Fees Under Fire: MLB Ticket Pricing Practices Face Scrutiny in Recent Consumer Class Actions
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
Three recent consumer class actions against Major League Baseball clubs are testing whether mandatory add-on charges on ticket purchases amount to deceptive pricing under state consumer protection laws. Filed between September 2025 and January 2026 against the Washington Nationals, Boston Red Sox, and San Francisco Giants, the complaints share a common theory: that tickets were marketed at base prices that excluded mandatory fees disclosed only at or near checkout.

The Lawsuits

In Gustafson v. Washington Nationals Baseball Club, LLC, No. 1:25-cv-03033 (D.D.C. filed Sept. 5, 2025), the plaintiff alleges that tickets purchased at Nationals Park in May 2023 were advertised at a base price but increased by roughly 60 percent after adding service charges, handling and convenience charges, and processing fees. According to the complaint those fees surfaced only after the transaction was completed or on credit card statements received after purchase. The suit proceeds under the D.C. Consumer Protection Procedures Act and seeks nationwide class relief dating back to 2022.

In Campagna v. Boston Red Sox Baseball Club, L.P., No. 1:26-cv-10182 (D. Mass. filed Jan. 16, 2026), three plaintiffs challenge both variable per-ticket fees and flat order fees allegedly revealed only at the final checkout screen. The complaint also alleges that countdown timers and layered terms and conditions pressured consumers to complete purchases before evaluating the total cost. The action proceeds under Massachusetts and Rhode Island consumer protection statutes and proposes a nationwide class dating to early 2022.

Most recently, in Flores v. San Francisco Baseball Associates LLC, No. 3:26-cv-00849 (N.D. Cal. filed Jan. 26, 2026), the plaintiff alleges that two $10 tickets to a Giants-Padres game in April 2024 ballooned to $29 after convenience and processing fees were added at the final purchase screen. The complaint asserts that this pricing structure continued until around July 1, 2024, when California’s Honest Pricing Act (SB 478) took effect. The plaintiff asserts claims under that statute as well as California’s Consumers Legal Remedies Act, Unfair Competition Law, and False Advertising Law.

The FTC Rule as Regulatory Backdrop

These lawsuits arrive just as the FTC’s Trade Regulation Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Fees (the “Junk Fees Rule”), codified at 16 C.F.R. Part 464, took effect. Published in the Federal Register on January 10, 2025 and effective May 12, 2025, the Rule makes it an unfair or deceptive practice to advertise a price for live-event tickets without clearly and conspicuously disclosing the total price, defined as the maximum amount a consumer must pay, including all mandatory fees. The total price must appear more prominently than other pricing information and must be disclosed before the consumer consents to pay.

The types of charges at issue in these MLB cases fall squarely within the Rule’s scope. The Rule does not create a private right of action and is not directly at issue in any of the three suits, which are grounded entirely in state consumer protection statutes. Nevertheless, it reinforces the plaintiffs’ central theory by establishing at the federal level the principle that an advertised ticket price should reflect the full mandatory cost.

Why These Cases Are Worth Watching

The legal theory here is not new. Whether excluding mandatory fees from an advertised price is materially misleading, and whether consumers relied on that representation, are familiar questions under state consumer protection law. What makes these cases worth watching is the broader context they fit into.

First, the regulatory environment has shifted underneath the live-event industry in a short period of time. California’s Honest Pricing Act took effect in July 2024. The FTC’s Junk Fees Rule followed in May 2025. Together, they represent a clear trend toward requiring all-in pricing for live-event tickets, and they give plaintiffs’ lawyers a federal regulatory framework to point to even in cases brought under state law. Whether courts treat the FTC Rule’s standards as informative on questions of materiality and deception in these state-law cases will be one thread to follow.

Second, all three complaints propose nationwide classes, which means they will raise choice-of-law and class manageability issues across state statutory schemes that vary considerably in their treatment of deceptive pricing. How courts handle those issues could shape the viability of future nationwide fee-transparency class actions beyond baseball and ticketing.

Third, these cases are part of a larger wave of scrutiny hitting the live-event ticketing industry from multiple directions. In March 2026, Live Nation Entertainment settled a long-running antitrust case with the Department of Justice, agreeing to cap service fees at Ticketmaster-operated amphitheaters at 15% of the ticket price and to open its platform to competing ticket sellers. That settlement was the product of years of public pressure that intensified after the Ticketmaster platform crashed during presales for Taylor Swift’s Eras Tour in late 2022. Between that settlement, the FTC Rule, state legislation like SB 478, and now private class actions targeting individual franchises, live-event ticket pricing is facing pressure from regulators, enforcers, and consumers simultaneously.

For sports franchises and other live-event sellers, the message is straightforward: pricing practices that separate mandatory fees from advertised ticket prices now carry risk from private litigation and federal regulatory enforcement at the same time. How courts handle these early MLB cases could set the tone for what follows.

This content is for general informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Trademark matters are fact-specific, and consultation with qualified counsel is recommended for individual circumstances.

item.title: USPTO Trademark Registration Series: Part 7 – Common Pitfalls in Trademark Registration
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
We conclude this series on trademark registration by examining where otherwise viable applications often go wrong. Many applicants encounter refusals or abandon applications due to preventable mistakes. Understanding these recurring pitfalls can save time and reduce costs and frustration. This article summarizes common errors, explains how applicants can overcome certain refusals, and flags emerging risks such as trademark scams.

Frequent Errors That Lead to Refusals or Delays

A large percentage of trademark applications encounter substantive or procedural issues during examination. According to one source citing USPTO data, only about 54 percent of applications filed in 2023 ultimately matured to registration, meaning nearly half faced refusals, abandonment, or other obstacles. Many of these outcomes result from avoidable oversights, including:

  • Inadequate pre-filing searches: Skipping a comprehensive clearance search remains one of the most common and costly mistakes. Without evaluating existing registrations and pending applications, applicants often face likelihood-of-confusion refusals under Section 2(d) of the Lanham Act, based on similarities in appearance, sound, meaning, or commercial impression.
  • Selection of weak or barred marks: Generic, merely descriptive, or geographically descriptive terms routinely trigger refusals. A term that directly names the goods or services, such as “Computer” for computers, can never function as a trademark and is not registrable under any circumstances.
  • Incorrect or incomplete application information: Errors in ownership are particularly dangerous. Listing the wrong owner, such as an individual instead of the controlling business entity, is frequently fatal and cannot always be corrected without refiling. Overbroad or inaccurate identifications of goods and services, incorrect classifications, and missing required information also delay or derail applications.
  • Defective specimens: For use-based filings, specimens must show the mark used in commerce as a source identifier, not merely as decoration or in mock-ups. Common refusals arise when applicants submit labels, screenshots, or promotional materials that fail to demonstrate trademark use.
  • Missed deadlines: Failure to respond to Office Actions within six months, file Statements of Use for intent-to-use applications, or submit post-registration maintenance filings results in abandonment, often without recourse.

Each of these mistakes increases the likelihood of delays, additional fees, or the need to start over entirely.

Refusals in Practice and How Some Are Overcome

Refusals are not the exception. Most applications receive at least one Office Action, and strategic responses can make the difference between abandonment and registration.

For example, the Washington Commanders’ trademark application initially received a Section 2(d) refusal in 2022 based on similarities to existing “Commanders” marks. Through targeted arguments distinguishing the marks and the relevant goods and services, the refusal was ultimately withdrawn and the application proceeded to registration.

Other refusals are resolved through evidentiary showings. Descriptive marks may be registered under Section 2(f) if the applicant can prove acquired distinctiveness through long-term use, advertising expenditures, and consumer recognition. Specimen refusals are frequently cured by submitting substitute evidence that clearly shows real-world use, such as product packaging or properly structured website displays.

However, not all refusals are overcome. For instance, generic marks rarely succeed, and willful errors can lead to permanent bars to registration.

No Guarantees and Fraud Risks

Trademark registration is never guaranteed. Even strong applications may face opposition or unexpected refusals. Fraud risks are real and severe. False specimens, inaccurate use claims, or misrepresentations can invalidate a registration years later.

Applicants should also remain alert to trademark scams. The USPTO regularly warns about imposters sending fake notices that mimic official correspondence and demand immediate payment, often via wire transfer, gift cards, or payment apps. Legitimate USPTO communications never require urgent payment or threaten immediate loss of rights without formal process. Application status should always be verified through the USPTO’s Trademark Status and Document Retrieval system.

Final Takeaway

Trademark registration is a powerful brand protection tool, but it rewards preparation and discipline. Avoidable mistakes remain the leading cause of refusals and abandonment. By understanding common pitfalls, engaging qualified counsel when appropriate, and staying current on procedural changes, applicants can materially improve their odds of securing durable trademark rights. For most businesses, informed strategy at the outset is the most cost-effective decision they can make.

This content is for general informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Trademark matters are fact-specific, and consultation with qualified counsel is recommended for individual circumstances.

item.title: Two Quarterback Trademark Strategies Signal the Future of Athlete Branding
item.dc:creator: alecmcniff
item.content:encoded:
On March 16, Caleb Williams Holding Inc. filed four trademark applications with the USPTO. Eleven days later, Fernando Mendoza filed two of his own. Together, these filings represent a new generation of NFL quarterbacks treating intellectual property as a core part of their business, even in its infancy.

Williams, the Bears' rising third-year quarterback, filed on an intent-to-use basis for two marks tied to the nickname “Iceman,” which he earned during a 2025 season that featured six fourth-quarter comebacks and an electric wild card win over Green Bay. His other two applications protect a silhouette of him mid-throw, based on an improbable fourth-and-eight conversion during that playoff game. His filings cover a wide range of goods and services, including eyewear, sunglasses, downloadable digital trading cards, apparel, athletic sporting goods, footballs, water bottles, mugs, bags, and more. These are not Williams’ first trademark filing, as he already has applications on record for his name, a “CW” logo, a bear claw logo, and additional silhouettes. In total, Williams now has eleven trademark applications either pending or in process at the USPTO.

Mendoza, the reigning Heisman and presumptive No. 1 pick in this month’s draft, filed two applications on March 27 covering his full name and his surname for athletic apparel. Although the filings are narrower in scope than Williams’, the instinct to protect his brand early is the same.

The business case for filing early

The traditional path for professional athletes was to build a career, let the endorsement deals come to you, and maybe think about trademarks later, if at all. Players filing trademarks during their playing careers—let alone before even beginning their professional careers—just wasn’t something that was normal. The brand belonged to the league, the team, or whoever was paying for the endorsement.

That model left value on the table, and athletes exposed. If someone else filed first, the player was forced to either buy back rights or fight an expensive legal battle to reclaim them. 

Williams and Mendoza are taking a more proactive approach by filing before their commercial value fully materializes. This secures a federal priority date, which is the legal equivalent of planting a flag. For Williams, that means staking a claim to “Iceman” while the nickname is still fresh and before third parties try to capitalize on it. For Mendoza, it means protecting his name before he goes from college quarterback to the face of an NFL franchise, a transition that will generate even more commercial interest than he already has.

What this means going forward

Trademark protection is no longer an afterthought reserved for players with existing lucrative shoe deals and production companies. Instead, it’s becoming routine for elite prospects and young professionals alike, and will likely trickle down into the college game, as athletes seek to maximize the value of their brand through NIL deals. Going forward, the athletes who treat their IP the way they treat their training, with intention, professional guidance, and an eye toward long-term value, will be the ones best positioned to control their commercial futures. By contrast, those who wait will end up paying more, fighting harder, and owning less.

This content is for general informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Trademark matters are fact-specific, and consultation with qualified counsel is recommended for individual circumstances.
